perturbation.original_text: Information labeled “ Confidential BCSI ” shall be treated and protected in accordance with the enforceable version of the NERC CIP -011 Cyber Security – Information Protection standard and shall require a Recipient background check and Recipient’s successful completion of VELCO -designated training in addition to compliance with the other requirements of and protections in this Agreement ; and
perturbation.changed_text: Information labeled “ Confidential BCSI ” shall be treated and protected in accordance with the other requirements of and protections in this Agreement ; and
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to NERC CIP-011 and the mandatory background check/training, the agreement now fails to explicitly require compliance with a critical cybersecurity standard. The agreement still protects the information, but less stringently.
perturbation.law_explanation: NERC CIP-011-2 mandates specific cybersecurity measures for protecting BES Cyber Systems Information (BCSI). Removing the reference to this standard weakens the protection of BCSI, potentially violating the intent, if not the explicit letter, of the standard. The edited NDA no longer explicitly requires the recipient to adhere to NERC CIP-011-2 standards.
perturbation.location: Section I V: Use an d Protection of Classified Information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 CFR § 39.11 - Reliability reports. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 39.11 - Reliability reports. CFR prev | next § 39.11 Reliability reports. (a) The Electric Reliability Organization shall conduct assessments as determined by the Commission of the reliability of the Bulk-Power System in North America and provide a report to the Commission and provide subsequent reports of the same to the Commission . (b) The Electric Reliability Organization shall conduct assessments of the adequacy of the Bulk-Power System in North America and report its findings to the Commission , the Secretary of Energy, each Regional Entity , and each Regional Advisory Body annually or more frequently if so ordered by the Commission . (c) The Electric Reliability Organization shall make available to the Commission , on a non-public and ongoing basis, access to the Transmission Availability Data System, Generator Availability Data System, and protection system misoperations databases, or any successor databases thereto. Such access will be limited to: (1) Data regarding U.S. facilities; and (2) Data that is required to be provided to the ERO. [Order 672, 71 FR 8736 , Feb. 17, 2006, as amended by Order 824, 81 FR 45008 , July 12, 2016] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly references and incorporates the NERC CIP-011 cybersecurity standard, including background checks and training. The changed text removes this explicit requirement, thereby weakening the mandatory safeguards for 'Confidential BCSI'. The Law Explanation clearly states NERC CIP-011-2 mandates specific cybersecurity measures for protecting BES Cyber Systems Information (BCSI), and that removing this reference potentially violates the intent, if not the explicit letter, of the standard. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly discuss CIP-011, the omission of CIP-011 in the modified text, coupled with the understanding that CIP-011 is the required standard for protecting BCSI as stated in the Law Explanation, creates a contradiction. The changed agreement is now less stringent and enforceable regarding cybersecurity measures for protecting BCSI which creates a structural flaw and thus a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall limit access to Classified Information to other Recipient s who also have the right pursuant to an agreement with VELCO to access the identical material. Any notes made by Recipient containing Classified Information shall be marked and designated in accordance with
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall limit access to Classified Information to any individual the Recipient deems appropriate. Any notes made by Recipient containing Classified Information do not need to be marked and designated in accordance with
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires recipients to limit access to those with agreements with VELCO and to properly mark notes. This has been changed to allow free access and removal of marking requirements, contradicting standard security practices for confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the spirit, if not the explicit letter, of trade secret protections and exemptions under FOIA (5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(4)). While not directly a FOIA violation (as this is a private agreement), the relaxation of access controls undermines the reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality, a key element in establishing trade secret status and thus eligibility for exemption from mandatory disclosure under FOIA if the information were to come into the government's possession. By weakening these protections, the likelihood of the information being disclosed increases.
perturbation.location: Section I V: Use an d Protection of Classified Information

Public information; agency rules, opinions, orders, records, and proceedings (a) Each agency shall make available to the public information as follows: (1) Each agency shall
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates restricted access to Classified Information to those with agreements with VELCO and requires proper marking of notes. The changed text allows access to "any individual the Recipient deems appropriate" and removes the marking requirement. This directly contradicts the 'reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality,' which the Law Explanation indicates is crucial for trade secret protection and eligibility for FOIA exemption. The modified text weakens security measures, potentially increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction as the organization of the contract affects its clarity or enforceability, leading to a required legal disclosure being misleading by allowing too broad of access.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Recipient is required to disclose C lassified Information by subpoena, law or other directive of a court, administrative agency , or arbitration panel, the Recipient will provide VELCO with immediate notice of such request in order to enable VELCO (and Recipient shall cooperate fully with VELCO) to se ek an appropriate protective order or other remedy . Recipient shall also consult with VELCO to identify steps available t o quash or narrow the scope of such request or legal process .
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Recipient is required to disclose C lassified Information by subpoena, law or other directive of a court, administrative agency, or arbitration panel, the Recipient is authorized to comply immediately with the request. VELCO is not to be notified.
perturbation.explanation: The original text required notification to VELCO and cooperation in seeking a protective order. The modified text eliminates these requirements, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosure of confidential information without VELCO's knowledge or opportunity to protect it.
perturbation.law_explanation: This alteration could indirectly lead to violations of 18 U.S.C. § 1905, which prohibits the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information obtained by federal employees or agencies. While the Recipient may not be a federal entity, their actions in disclosing information without notifying VELCO and allowing them to seek a protective order could result in the information ending up in the hands of a federal agency and subsequently being disclosed in violation of this statute. The original clause ensured VELCO had a chance to prevent that chain of events.
perturbation.location: Section I V: Use an d Protection of Classified Information

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next Whoever, being an officer or employee of the United States or of any department or agency thereof, any person acting on behalf of the Federal Housing Finance Agency , or agent of the Department of Justice as defined in the Antitrust Civil Process Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1311–131 4), or being an employee of a private sector organization who is or was assigned to an agency under chapter 37 of title 5, publishes, divulges, discloses, or makes known in any manner or to any extent not authorized by law any information coming to him in the course of his employment or official duties or by reason of any examination or investigation made by, or return, report or record made to or filed with, such department or agency or officer or employee thereof, which information concerns or relates to the trade secrets, processes, operations, style of work, or apparatus, or to the identity, confidential statistical data, amount or source of any income, profits, losses, or expenditures of any person, firm, partnership, corporation, or association; or permits any income return or copy thereof or any book containing any abstract or particulars thereof to be seen or examined by any person except as provided by law; shall be fined under this title, or imprisoned not more than one year, or both; and shall be removed from office or employment. L. 104–294, title VI, § 601(a)(8) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3498 ; Pub. L. 107–347, title II, § 209(d)(2) , Dec. 17, 2002 , 116 Stat. 2930 ; Pub. L. 110–289, div. A, title I, §
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original contract's intent to protect confidential information, potentially leading to violations of 18 U.S.C. § 1905. The original text mandated notifying VELCO and cooperating to seek protective orders, while the changed text authorizes immediate compliance without notification. Although the law snippets explicitly address 'officer or employee of the United States,' the LLM explanation suggests that unauthorized disclosure could indirectly involve a federal agency, which the original clause aimed to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Vermont without regard to its conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China without regard to its conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies Vermont law. Changing this to the law of the People's Republic of China introduces uncertainty and potential unenforceability, especially if the contract concerns matters within the United States. Chinese law may offer significantly different protections for confidential information, potentially harming VELCO.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the principles of choice of law as outlined in Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. While parties are generally free to choose the governing law of their contract, the chosen law must have a reasonable relationship to the parties or the subject matter of the contract. Choosing Chinese law for a contract between a Vermont company and another US entity, involving the protection of US energy infrastructure information, lacks a reasonable basis and could be deemed invalid, especially if it undermines fundamental policies of the state with the most significant relationship to the transaction (Vermont). This could render the entire agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Vermont law, while the changed text specifies the law of the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation states this "lacks a reasonable basis and could be deemed invalid, especially if it undermines fundamental policies of the state with the most significant relationship to the transaction (Vermont)." The Perturbation Explanation notes that "Chinese law may offer significantly different protections for confidential information, potentially harming VELCO." The Wex definition of conflict of laws indicates that a court determines what law to apply, and the conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. Therefore, switching to Chinese law introduces a legal contradiction, as it may not have a reasonable relationship to the parties or subject matter, potentially undermining the original intent and enforceability of the agreement within the U.S.
